summary: The q8 dataset contains 10 problems (q1-q10), each showing exactly 100 issues, indicating uniform activity levels with no single problem standing out by raw volume. The provided context lacks differentiated metrics such as issue type, resolution rate, or recency, so traction and solvability cannot be reliably distinguished from the data alone. Additional metadata is required to produce confident prioritization beyond surface-level observations.
problems: All 10 problems (q1-q10) report an identical count of 100 issues, suggesting either a data cap, a default/placeholder value, or genuinely uniform activity that obscures real differences.
No issue categorization (open vs. resolved, error type, severity) is available, preventing identification of common failure patterns.
The 'Issues Per Problem' section is empty and 'Proof Status' only lists counts, so proof completion or solvability signals are absent.
No recency or trend data is provided, making it impossible to assess which problems have recent momentum.
Uniform 100-issue counts may indicate a truncation or logging artifact rather than meaningful research activity.
highlights: Comprehensive coverage: all 10 problems are being actively tracked with issue logs in place.
Consistent data structure across problems enables straightforward comparative analysis once richer metrics are added.
A substantial issue corpus (1,000 total) offers a strong foundation for pattern mining and automated triage.

suggested_todos.title: Verify whether the uniform '100 issues' value is a real count or a data cap/placeholder artifact
Enrich issue data with status (open/closed), category, and severity to enable meaningful prioritization
Add recency and trend metrics to identify which problems have genuine momentum
Cluster issues by failure type to surface common patterns across q1-q10
Define proof-status indicators (e.g., partial proof, verified steps) to estimate solvability
Re-run this insight report once differentiated metrics are available
